lessons.title: May & might — possibility
lessons.description: Use may and might to talk about things that are possible but not certain in the future.

content.title: May and might — expressing possibility
content.body:
We use MAY and MIGHT to talk about things that are POSSIBLE — not certain.

MAY = slightly more certain (about 50% chance)
MIGHT = slightly less certain (about 30-40% chance)
In everyday speech, they are often interchangeable.

Examples:
• I may go to the party — I haven't decided yet.
• It might rain this evening — carry an umbrella.
• She might get the job — she interviewed well.
• We may not make it on time — the traffic is bad.

Negative:
• I may not come tomorrow.
• He might not know the answer.
content.grammar_note:
may/might + BASE VERB (no 'to', no -ing)
Negative: may not / might not (mayn't is rare; mightn't is informal)
No 's' for he/she/it — may/might never change form

content.title: May/might vs will vs going to
content.body:
Compare these levels of certainty:

100% certain:
'I will be there at 6.' (definite)

High certainty (decided plan):
'I'm going to be there at 6.' (already decided)

Possible (not certain):
'I may/might be there at 6.' (not sure yet)

Key tip: When you are NOT SURE if something will happen, use MAY or MIGHT — not WILL.

Wrong: 'I will maybe go.' ✗
Right: 'I might go.' ✓
Right: 'I may go.' ✓
content.grammar_note:
will = certain prediction / decided
going to = decided plan / evidence
may/might = possible, not certain

vocabulary.example: I'm uncertain about my weekend plans.

vocabulary.meaning: a chance that something might happen
vocabulary.example: There's a possibility of rain tonight.
She hesitated before accepting the offer.

vocabulary.meaning: to think about something before deciding
vocabulary.example: I'm considering whether to apply or not.

vocabulary.meaning: to be decided by something else
vocabulary.example: It depends on the weather.
It's unlikely to snow in Chennai!

sentences: I might visit my parents this weekend — I haven't decided yet.
She may not come to the meeting — she's unwell.
It might get cold tonight — take a jacket.
We may have to reschedule — the venue isn't confirmed.
He might be the right person for the job.

content.question: Priya isn't sure if she can attend the event on Saturday. What does she say?
options: I might attend on Saturday — it depends on my family plans.
content.correct: I might attend on Saturday — it depends on my family plans.
content.explanation: MIGHT expresses possibility/uncertainty — she hasn't decided and it's not certain.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses MAY for possibility?
content.correct: It may rain tonight.
content.explanation: MAY + BASE VERB (no -ing, no 'to', no 's'). 'May rain' is correct.

content.question: Arjun says: 'I'm not 100% sure, but there's a good chance I'll get the promotion.' Which best expresses this?
options: I will definitely get the promotion.
I'm getting the promotion next week.
content.correct: I may get the promotion.
content.explanation: MAY = possible but not certain — exactly what 'not 100% sure but good chance' means.

content.question: What is the difference between 'I might come' and 'I'm coming'?
options: No difference — both mean the same thing.
'I might come' = possible, not sure. 'I'm coming' = fixed, arranged plan.
'I might come' = very certain. 'I'm coming' = uncertain.
'I might come' is past tense. 'I'm coming' is present tense.
content.correct: 'I might come' = possible, not sure. 'I'm coming' = fixed, arranged plan.
content.explanation: This is a key distinction: might = uncertain possibility; present continuous for future = confirmed arrangement.

content.instruction: Fill in with MAY, MIGHT, MAY NOT, or MIGHT NOT.

sentences.text: I ___ go to Ooty next month — it depends on whether I get leave approved.
She ___ be in the office today — she was feeling sick yesterday.
The results ___ be announced tomorrow or it ___ take until next week.
Don't wait for me for dinner — I ___ be late. The traffic is terrible.

content.instruction: Rewrite using MAY or MIGHT to make the sentence less certain.

sentences.text: It will rain this afternoon. → It ___ this afternoon.
She will get the promotion. → She ___ the promotion.
They won't finish on time. → They ___ not finish on time.
He will move to Singapore. → He ___ to Singapore.

content.instruction: Arrange into a possibility sentence.
content.answer: I might apply for that job — not sure yet.

content.instruction: Arrange into a negative possibility sentence.
content.answer: We may not make it to the flight in time.

content.instruction: Match the uncertain situation to the best MAY/MIGHT sentence.

pairs.right: It might rain this evening.

pairs.left: Boss might give promotion — not decided
pairs.right: She may get a promotion this quarter.

pairs.left: Not sure if friend will join dinner
pairs.right: Meera might not come — she seemed busy.

pairs.left: Not certain the train will be on time
pairs.right: The train may be delayed.

pairs.left: Considering a holiday but not booked
pairs.right: I might go to Kerala in December.

content.title: Planning a trip with uncertainty — Pune
content.setting: Kavya and her friend Ravi are trying to plan a trip to Himachal Pradesh but are unsure about many things.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ravi, I've been thinking about that Himachal trip we discussed. Do you think it'll actually happen this year?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: It might! I may be able to take leave in October, but it depends on the project deadline.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: October sounds good — the weather might be perfect there. But what if it snows already?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: It may snow in higher areas like Spiti, but Manali and Kasol might still be okay in October.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Should we book early? Or might prices come down closer to the date?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Flights might get cheaper, but hotels may fill up fast in October — it's peak season. I'd book hotels early.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: True. What about Sonal — might she join us?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: She may come. She said she might take a long break in October, but she may not be able to afford it right now.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I see. And transport — should we rent a car?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: We might rent a car or we may just take cabs from Manali. Depends on the group size.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Okay, so nothing is confirmed yet?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Not yet! But it might all come together by next month. Let's decide by August.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Alright. It might be the best trip we've ever taken — I can feel it!

comprehension_questions.question: Why might Ravi not be able to go in October?
options: It depends on his project deadline.
comprehension_questions.correct: It depends on his project deadline.

comprehension_questions.question: Why does Ravi say to book hotels early?
options: Hotels may fill up fast — it's peak season.
comprehension_questions.correct: Hotels may fill up fast — it's peak season.

comprehension_questions.question: Why might Sonal not join the trip?
options: She may not be able to afford it right now.
comprehension_questions.correct: She may not be able to afford it right now.

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: She might to come to the party.
sentences.correct: She might come to the party.

sentences.explanation: MIGHT never changes — no 's' for any person.

sentences.wrong: I will maybe go to the market later.
sentences.correct: I might go to the market later. / I may go to the market later.
sentences.explanation: 'Will maybe' is not standard. Use MAY or MIGHT for uncertainty.

content.scenario: Your colleague asks if you'll attend the optional team outing on Friday. You'd like to go but you have a family commitment that might finish in time. What do you say?
options: I will come to the outing.
I'm not coming to the outing.
I might come — it depends on whether I finish my family commitment in time.
I will maybe come if I want.
content.correct: I might come — it depends on whether I finish my family commitment in time.
content.explanation: MIGHT is correct for genuine uncertainty. 'Will maybe' is not natural English.

content.instruction: Talk about 5 things in your life that are POSSIBLE but not certain in the next few months. Use may, might, may not, and might not.
content.prompt: Share things that might happen for you soon — plans, changes, possibilities you're considering.
content.example_response: There are several things in my life right now that are possible but not confirmed. First, I might get a transfer to our Hyderabad office — my manager mentioned it but nothing is decided. I may apply for a part-time online course in data science — I've been thinking about it for months but I'm not sure about the time commitment. My family might move to a bigger flat — we've been looking but haven't found the right place yet. My friend and I may go on a road trip to Coorg in September, but it depends on whether we both get leave approved at the same time. And I might finally start that YouTube channel I've been planning — I may not though, because I'm still a bit nervous about putting myself out there.

content.instruction: A friend asks you 5 questions about next week. Answer each one using may or might — because you're genuinely not certain about any of them.
content.prompt: Answer these 5 questions with uncertain responses: 1. Are you free next Saturday? 2. Will you come to the team lunch? 3. Will it be a busy week for you? 4. Are you going to the gym next week? 5. Will you be in the office on Friday?
content.example_response: Great questions! Let me answer honestly. Am I free next Saturday? I might be free — it depends on whether my cousin's visit from Coimbatore happens or not. She may come this weekend or she may postpone. Will I come to the team lunch? I may come if I finish my morning deliverables on time. I might be in back-to-back calls until 2 PM though, so it depends. Will it be a busy week? It might be very busy actually — we have a client review on Wednesday which might run long. Am I going to the gym? I may go on Tuesday and Thursday, but I might skip if work gets too hectic. And will I be in the office on Friday? I might work from home on Friday — my manager may allow it. So the honest answer to all five is: it depends!

content.instruction: Complete the weather forecast using MAY and MIGHT.
content.text:
Tomorrow's weather forecast for South India:

Chennai: Skies ___ (1) remain cloudy through the morning. There ___ (2) be light showers in the afternoon, especially in coastal areas. Temperatures ___ (3) drop slightly — carry a light jacket if you're going out in the evening.

Bangalore: The morning ___ (4) be pleasant with some sunshine. However, by evening, there ___ (5) be heavy rain — thunderstorms are possible. Outdoor plans ___ (6) need to be reconsidered.

Coimbatore: It ___ (7) be a dry day, but there's a small chance of drizzle near the Nilgiris. Visibility ___ (8) be low in the ghat sections after 4 PM — drive carefully.

content.title: Lesson 5 complete — May & might
key_points: MAY = possible (about 50% chance)
MIGHT = possible (slightly less certain — 30-40%)
Both: MAY/MIGHT + BASE VERB (no 'to', no -ing, no 's')
Negative: MAY NOT / MIGHT NOT
Use when NOT SURE — not 'I will maybe'
May and might are often interchangeable in everyday English
content.next_lesson: Possibility in conversation — hedging and expressing doubt

lessons.title: Possibility in conversation
lessons.description: Use hedging language and natural expressions to talk about uncertainty and possibility in real conversations.

content.title: Hedging language — sounding natural with uncertainty
content.body:
In natural conversation, we don't always speak with 100% certainty. We use 'hedging' language to soften what we say.

Common hedging expressions:
• I think... (mild opinion)
• I'm not sure but... (uncertainty)
• It's possible that... (formal possibility)
• There's a chance that... (possibility)
• I suppose... (reluctant agreement or mild thought)
• Probably / possibly / perhaps (adverbs of possibility)
• As far as I know... (based on my limited information)
• I imagine... (I believe but am not certain)
• Don't quote me on this, but... (informal — I'm not 100% sure)
content.grammar_note:
These phrases come BEFORE the main statement
Example: 'I think it might rain.' = double hedging (both think + might)

content.title: Responding to uncertain plans
content.body:
When someone gives an uncertain answer (with may/might), you can respond naturally:

Accepting uncertainty:
• 'No worries — let me know when you decide.'
• 'That's fine — just keep me updated.'
• 'Let's play it by ear.' (decide later based on how things go)

Asking for more info:
• 'What does it depend on?'
• 'Do you have a rough idea yet?'
• 'When do you think you'll know for sure?'

Keeping options open:
• 'Either way works for me.'
• 'I'll pencil you in for now.'
content.grammar_note:
'Let's play it by ear' = decide as things develop
'Pencil in' = put in the schedule but not final

vocabulary.example: I'm certain he will come.
It's likely to rain this evening.
It's unlikely they'll cancel.

vocabulary.meaning: probably not true or going to happen
vocabulary.example: It's doubtful she'll accept.
It'll take roughly two hours.
Approximately 50 people may attend.

sentences: I think it might be cancelled — but don't quote me on that.
As far as I know, the meeting is still on for tomorrow.
I'm not sure, but there's a chance the project will be delayed.
I suppose he might agree if you explain properly.
It's possible that they'll announce the results next week.

content.question: Your colleague asks if the client confirmed the deal. You don't know for sure. Which is the most natural response?
options: As far as I know, they're still considering — I'm not sure if they've decided yet.
content.correct: As far as I know, they're still considering — I'm not sure if they've decided yet.
content.explanation: 'As far as I know' + 'not sure' = honest hedging when you don't have certain information.

content.question: You're asked to estimate when a project will finish. You think it'll be about 3 weeks but you're not completely sure. What do you say?
options: It will finish in exactly 3 weeks.
I think it'll take roughly three weeks, but it might go a bit longer.
content.correct: I think it'll take roughly three weeks, but it might go a bit longer.
content.explanation: 'I think' + 'roughly' + 'might' = layered hedging. This sounds professional and honest.

content.question: Your friend invites you to a party but your plans aren't set. The most polite uncertain response is:
options: I'll probably come — I might need to check if my parents need me that evening.
content.correct: I'll probably come — I might need to check if my parents need me that evening.
content.explanation: 'Probably' + 'might need to check' = honest, polite, hedged response that keeps the option open.

content.question: What does 'let's play it by ear' mean?
options: Let's listen to music while we decide.
Let's plan everything in detail now.
Let's decide later depending on how things go.
Let's not make any plans at all.
content.correct: Let's decide later depending on how things go.
content.explanation: 'Play it by ear' is an idiom meaning: don't fix the plan now, decide as the situation develops.

content.instruction: Add the correct hedging phrase from the box: [I think, probably, as far as I know, I suppose, there's a chance]

sentences.text: ___ the office will be closed tomorrow — it's a public holiday.
It'll ___ take about two hours to reach there by metro.
___ that the concert might be cancelled if it rains heavily.
___ she might accept the offer — it's a good salary.

content.instruction: Make these certain sentences sound less certain using hedging words.

sentences.text: He knows the answer. → ___ he might know the answer.
The train will be on time. → The train will ___ be on time.
They will launch the product in March. → ___ they may launch in March.
She's the best candidate. → ___ she might be the best candidate.

content.instruction: Arrange into a hedged sentence.
content.answer: I think it'll probably be crowded tomorrow.

content.instruction: Arrange into a natural uncertain response.
content.answer: As far as I know, the deal is still on.

content.instruction: Match the hedging phrase to its meaning.

pairs.right: Based on what I currently know
Very likely but not 100% sure
I believe, but not strongly
I'm not certain — please verify
We'll decide as things develop

content.title: Discussing uncertain work news — IT office, Chennai
content.setting: Meera and Arjun are colleagues talking about uncertain news about their company's upcoming restructuring.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Meera, have you heard anything about the restructuring? People are saying our team might be affected.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I've heard some things, but don't quote me on this — as far as I know, they may merge two teams.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Which teams? Do you know?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I think it might be our team and the analytics team, but I'm not certain. I suppose we'll know by Friday.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Do you think anyone will lose their job?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: It's possible. There's a chance they might reduce headcount, but it's also possible they'll just reorganise roles.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: That's worrying. What should we do?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I'd probably update your resume — just in case. It might not come to that, but it's better to be prepared.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Good advice. Do you think the manager will tell us anything in Monday's meeting?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I imagine she might give us some indication. But I wouldn't expect full details yet — these things take time.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Should I ask her directly?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You could — but she may not have all the answers yet either. I think we'll just have to play it by ear for now.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: You're right. No point panicking over something that might not even happen.

comprehension_questions.question: What does Meera think might happen to the teams?
options: The teams might move to a new office.

comprehension_questions.question: What does Meera advise Arjun to probably do?
What does 'play it by ear' mean in this context?
options: Wait and decide based on how things develop
Speak to the manager every day
comprehension_questions.correct: Wait and decide based on how things develop

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: I think probably it might will rain.
sentences.correct: I think it might probably rain. / I think it will probably rain.
sentences.explanation: Don't combine 'might' and 'will' — use one modal. 'Probably' sits between the subject and verb or after the modal.

sentences.wrong: As far as I know, they might to postpone the meeting.
sentences.correct: As far as I know, they might postpone the meeting.

sentences.wrong: There's a possibility it rains tomorrow.
sentences.correct: There's a possibility it will rain / might rain tomorrow.
sentences.explanation: After 'there's a possibility', use WILL or MIGHT + base verb.

content.scenario: A new colleague asks you if the company gives annual bonuses. You received one last year but you're not sure if it's a fixed policy. What do you say?
options: Yes, you'll definitely get a bonus.
As far as I know, they do give bonuses — I received one last year, but I'm not sure if it's guaranteed every year. You might want to check with HR.
content.correct: As far as I know, they do give bonuses — I received one last year, but I'm not sure if it's guaranteed every year. You might want to check with HR.
content.explanation: This response is honest and helpful — it uses hedging ('as far as I know', 'not sure', 'might want to check').

content.instruction: Someone asks you about the future of your industry or career field over the next 10 years. Give a hedged, thoughtful response using possibility language.
content.prompt: Talk about what might happen in your industry or career field in the next 10 years. Use hedging language throughout.
content.example_response: I work in the IT sector, so there are quite a few things I think might change in the next 10 years. First, I imagine AI will probably take over a lot of routine coding tasks — things like testing and debugging might become largely automated. I suppose that could be good or bad depending on your role. As far as I know, companies are already investing heavily in AI tools. There's a strong possibility that cloud computing will become even more central — most businesses may move entirely off on-premise servers. I think cybersecurity might become one of the most important and well-paid fields. It's also possible that remote work will remain common — maybe even more so. I don't think jobs in tech will disappear entirely though. There'll probably always be a need for creative problem-solvers. I suppose the key is to keep learning and adapting — whatever happens.

content.instruction: Describe your ideal but uncertain future. Where might you be living in 5 years? What might your life look like? Use lots of hedging language.
content.prompt: Talk about where you might be and what your life might look like in 5 years. Be honest about what's uncertain.
content.example_response: In five years, I honestly don't know exactly where I'll be — and I think that's okay! I suppose I might still be in Chennai, or I might have moved to Bangalore or even abroad for work. As far as I know, there are good opportunities growing in Hyderabad too, so that's also possible. I imagine I'll probably have advanced in my career — maybe into a senior or lead role. I think I might be working in a hybrid setup, partly from home and partly in an office. There's a chance I may have started a side project or small business by then — I've been thinking about it for a while. On the personal side, I might be married, I might not be — it depends on how things go. I'd like to probably travel more — maybe visit Japan or Europe at some point. I suppose the honest answer is: a lot might change, and I'm open to most of it!

content.instruction: Complete this WhatsApp-style conversation using hedging language from the lesson.
content.text:
Priya: Hey! Are you coming to Meera's birthday dinner on Saturday?

Kavya: ___ (1) I'll come — I have a family thing in the morning but it should finish by 4. I ___ (2) be a bit late though.

Priya: No worries! What time do you think you'll arrive?

Kavya: ___ (3), around 8? But don't hold a seat — ___ (4) I might be delayed by traffic.

Priya: That's fine! ___ (5) we might go to dessert somewhere after — are you okay with that?

Kavya: ___ (6) yes! ___ (7) there are some good places near the restaurant. Let's ___ (8) and decide on dessert when we're there.

Priya: Perfect. See you Saturday — hopefully!
answers: as far as I know / I think

content.title: Lesson 6 complete — Possibility in conversation
key_points: Hedging = softening what you say when uncertain
Common hedges: I think, I suppose, I imagine, as far as I know, probably
Combine hedges for more natural speech: 'I think it'll probably...'
Useful phrases: 'let's play it by ear', 'don't quote me on this', 'there's a chance'
Don't say 'will maybe' — use might/may instead
content.next_lesson: Writing formal emails — structure and language

lessons.title: Writing formal emails — structure and language
lessons.description: Learn how to write clear, polite formal emails in English — greeting, purpose, body, and sign-off.

content.title: The structure of a formal email
content.body:
A formal email has five parts:

1. SUBJECT LINE — short and clear
   'Request for Leave — 12-14 March'

2. GREETING
   'Dear Mr Sharma,' / 'Dear Ms Priya,' / 'Dear Sir/Madam,'

3. OPENING LINE — state your reason
   'I am writing to request...' / 'I am writing regarding...'

4. BODY — your main message (1-3 short paragraphs)

5. CLOSING + SIGN-OFF
   'I look forward to hearing from you.'
   'Yours sincerely,' / 'Kind regards,' / 'Best regards,'
   [Your name]
content.grammar_note:
Formal emails use full forms: I am (not I'm), I do not (not I don't)
Avoid casual phrases: 'Hey!', 'Thanks a ton', 'Cheers'

content.title: Key formal email phrases
content.body:
OPENING LINES:
• I am writing to inform you that...
• I am writing with regard to...
• Further to our conversation, I would like to...
• I am contacting you regarding...

MAKING REQUESTS:
• I would be grateful if you could...
• Could you please...?
• I would appreciate it if...
• Please find attached...

CLOSING LINES:
• I look forward to hearing from you.
• Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any questions.
• Thank you for your time and consideration.
• I await your response.
content.grammar_note:
Use 'would' for polite requests — more formal than 'can'
'I would be grateful' > 'I want'

vocabulary.example: I am writing regarding your recent application.
Please find the report attached.
I would be grateful for your prompt response.

vocabulary.meaning: to pause/be unsure before doing something
vocabulary.example: Please do not hesitate to contact me.
Please reply at your earliest convenience.
Yours sincerely, Priya Rajan

sentences: I am writing to request a meeting at your earliest convenience.
Please find the completed form attached to this email.
I would be grateful if you could confirm receipt of this message.
Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any questions.
I look forward to hearing from you.

content.question: Which is the correct formal email greeting when you know the person's name?
content.correct: Dear Ms Priya,
content.explanation: 'Dear + title + name' is the standard formal greeting. 'Hey' and 'Hi there' are too casual.

content.question: Which is the best opening line for a formal email requesting a meeting?
options: I am writing to request a brief meeting at your convenience.
Just wanted to check if we can chat.
content.correct: I am writing to request a brief meeting at your convenience.
content.explanation: 'I am writing to...' is the standard formal opening. The others are too casual or vague.

content.question: Which closing line is most appropriate for a formal email?
options: I look forward to hearing from you. Kind regards,
content.correct: I look forward to hearing from you. Kind regards,
content.explanation: 'I look forward to hearing from you' + 'Kind regards' = professional and standard formal closing.

content.question: Ravi wants to ask his professor if she received his assignment. Which is the most appropriate formal sentence?
options: I wanted to check — did you see my work?
I am writing to confirm whether you received my assignment submission.
Just checking if my assignment reached you!
content.correct: I am writing to confirm whether you received my assignment submission.
content.explanation: Formal structure + polite language = 'I am writing to confirm whether...' This is professional and respectful.

content.instruction: Complete the formal email phrases.

sentences.text: I am writing ___ regard ___ your job application.
Please find the updated document ___ to this email.
I would be ___ if you could confirm the date.
Please do not ___ to contact me if you have any further questions.

content.instruction: Make these informal phrases formal.

sentences.text: I want to know about the job. → I am writing ___ regarding the position.
Can you send the file? → I ___ be grateful if you could send the file.
I'll wait for your reply. → I ___ forward ___ hearing from you.
Thanks a lot! → ___ you for your time and consideration.

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct formal email opening sentence.
content.answer: I am writing to request a brief meeting.

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct formal email closing sentence.
content.answer: I look forward to hearing from you.

content.instruction: Match the informal phrase to its formal equivalent.

pairs.right: I am writing to enquire about...
I would be grateful if you could assist me.
Please find the document attached.
I look forward to hearing from you.
Thank you for your time and consideration.

content.title: Discussing how to write a formal email — college, Bangalore
content.setting: Sonal is helping her friend Arjun write a formal email to a professor requesting an extension on his assignment.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Sonal, I need to write a formal email to Professor Verma asking for an assignment extension. I've never written one before.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Okay, it's not too difficult. First, write a clear subject line — something like 'Request for Assignment Extension — Arjun Mehta'.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Okay. Then how do I start?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Start with 'Dear Professor Verma,' — never 'Hey' or just her first name in a formal email.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Got it. And then?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Your opening line should state why you're writing. Something like: 'I am writing to request an extension for the Marketing Case Study assignment.'

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Simple and to the point. What do I put in the body?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Explain your reason briefly — keep it honest and professional. Something like: 'I have been unwell this week and have been unable to complete the work to the required standard.'

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Should I mention the new date I want?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Yes — say exactly what you need: 'I would be grateful if you could grant me an extension until Friday, 14th March.'

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Perfect. And how do I end it?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: End with: 'I apologise for any inconvenience caused and I look forward to your response.' Then sign off with 'Yours sincerely' or 'Kind regards' and your full name.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: This is really helpful. Should I say anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: You could add: 'Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require any further information.' That shows you're being professional and cooperative.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Great! I'll write it up now. Thanks so much, Sonal.

comprehension_questions.question: What should Arjun use as the subject line?
options: Request for Assignment Extension — Arjun Mehta
comprehension_questions.correct: Request for Assignment Extension — Arjun Mehta

comprehension_questions.question: Why is Arjun requesting an extension?

content.instruction: This formal email has 3 mistakes. Find and correct them.

sentences.wrong: Hey Mr Kapoor, I'm writing to ask about the job.
sentences.correct: Dear Mr Kapoor, I am writing to enquire about the position.
sentences.explanation: 'Hey' is too casual for a formal email. Use 'Dear'. Also use full forms: 'I am' not 'I'm'.

sentences.wrong: Can you please tell me when the results will come?
sentences.correct: I would be grateful if you could inform me when the results will be announced.
sentences.explanation: 'Can you' is informal. Use 'I would be grateful if you could...' in formal emails.

sentences.correct: Thank you for your time. Kind regards, Ravi Kumar
sentences.explanation: 'Thanks a lot' and 'Cheers' are too informal. Use 'Thank you' and 'Kind regards' or 'Yours sincerely'.

content.scenario: You are writing to a hotel in Mysore to ask if they have rooms available for the Dussehra weekend. What is the best subject line?
options: Room Availability Enquiry — Dussehra Weekend (12-14 October)
content.correct: Room Availability Enquiry — Dussehra Weekend (12-14 October)
content.explanation: A good formal email subject line is specific, clear, and professional. Include what + when.

content.instruction: Read this formal email aloud and then explain each part — what is the subject, greeting, opening line, body, and closing?
content.prompt:
Read and explain the structure of this email:

Subject: Request for Meeting — Project Proposal

Dear Ms Kavitha,

I am writing to request a brief meeting to discuss my project proposal for the upcoming product launch.

I would be grateful if you could spare 20 minutes at your earliest convenience. I am available on Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon.

Please find a summary of the proposal attached.

I look forward to hearing from you. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require any further information.

Yours sincerely,
Arjun Mehta
content.example_response: This is a well-structured formal email. The subject line is 'Request for Meeting — Project Proposal' — it's short, clear and tells the reader exactly what the email is about. The greeting is 'Dear Ms Kavitha' — formal and respectful, using the title and name. The opening line 'I am writing to request a brief meeting' immediately states the purpose — this is very important in formal emails. The body has two short paragraphs: the first makes the request and explains how long it will take, and the second offers specific availability — Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon — which makes it easy for the reader to respond. There's also a mention of an attachment. The closing lines are very professional: 'I look forward to hearing from you' is the standard formal closing, and 'please do not hesitate to contact me' shows politeness. The sign-off 'Yours sincerely' followed by full name is perfectly formal.

content.instruction: You need to write a formal email to your company's HR department requesting a work-from-home arrangement for the next month. Describe what you would write — include all 5 parts of the email.
content.prompt: Dictate a formal email to HR requesting a work-from-home arrangement. Include subject, greeting, reason, request, and sign-off.
content.example_response:
Here is the formal email I would write:

Subject: Request for Work-From-Home Arrangement — November 2025

Dear HR Team,

I am writing to request a work-from-home arrangement for the month of November 2025. I am currently managing a health situation that requires me to stay close to home during this period, though I am fully able to continue my work responsibilities remotely.

I would be grateful if you could grant this arrangement for the period of 1st to 30th November. I assure you that my availability, productivity and response times will not be affected. I am happy to attend any mandatory in-person meetings with sufficient notice.

I have attached a brief note from my doctor for your reference.

Thank you for your understanding. I look forward to your response at your earliest convenience. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require any further information.

Yours sincerely,
Priya Rajan
Software Engineer, Team Horizon

content.instruction: Complete this formal email with the correct words/phrases.
content.text:
Subject: ___ (1) for Leave of Absence — 3-5 April

Dear Mr Sharma,

I am ___ (2) to request three days of leave from 3rd to 5th April 2025, due to a family function.

I have ensured that all my current deliverables ___ (3) be completed before my leave begins. I will also ensure a proper handover ___ (4) my colleague Ms Meera Iyer.

I ___ (5) be grateful if you could kindly approve this request. I am happy to discuss this ___ (6) your convenience.

Thank you for your time. I ___ (7) forward to your response.

___ (8) sincerely,
Kavya Nair

content.title: Lesson 7 complete — Writing formal emails
key_points: 5 parts: Subject line, Greeting (Dear...), Opening line, Body, Closing + Sign-off
Use full forms: I am, I do not, I would (not I'm, don't, I'd) — though contractions are acceptable in less formal professional emails
Opening: 'I am writing to...'
Request: 'I would be grateful if you could...'
Closing: 'I look forward to hearing from you.'
Sign-off: 'Yours sincerely / Kind regards / Best regards'

lessons.title: Formal vs informal email language
lessons.description: Understand and practise the difference between formal and informal register in written English.

content.title: Register — formal vs informal
content.body:
REGISTER means the level of formality in your language. You choose based on WHO you are writing to.

FORMAL (to a boss, client, professor, stranger, company):
• Full sentences, full forms
• No slang, no emoji
• Polite and distant
• 'I am writing to request...'

INFORMAL (to a friend, close colleague, family):
• Contractions, casual phrases
• Can use emoji, exclamation marks
• Friendly and warm
• 'Hey! Just wanted to check...'

SEMI-FORMAL (to a known colleague you're friendly with):
• Mix of both — friendly but professional
content.grammar_note:
Register mistakes = mismatches
Writing to your boss like a friend = too casual
Writing to your friend like a formal letter = awkward

content.title: Formal vs informal phrase comparison
content.body:
INFORMAL → FORMAL

• Hey! → Dear [Name],
• Just wanted to say → I am writing to inform you
• Can you...? → Could you please...? / I would be grateful if you could...
• Sorry for the late reply → I apologise for the delay in responding
• Thanks! → Thank you for your time
• No problem → It is not an inconvenience
• I'll do it → I will ensure it is completed
• ASAP → at your earliest convenience
• FYI → For your information / Please note that
• Let me know → Please do not hesitate to contact me
• Bye! / Cheers → Yours sincerely / Kind regards
content.grammar_note:
Abbreviations like FYI, ASAP, BTW are informal only
Never use them in formal emails

vocabulary.example: All correspondence should be kept on file.
Please acknowledge receipt of this email.
I apologise for any inconvenience.

vocabulary.meaning: to send a received email to someone else
vocabulary.example: I will forward the email to the relevant team.

vocabulary.meaning: carbon copy — to send a copy to another person
vocabulary.example: Please cc the manager on all client emails.
This is urgent — please reply today.

sentences: I apologise for the delay in responding to your email.
Could you please confirm your availability for a meeting?
Please note that the deadline has been extended to Friday.
I will ensure the report is completed by end of day.
Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention.

content.question: Priya is writing to a potential employer. Which greeting is correct?
content.correct: Dear Mr Rajesh Kumar,
content.explanation: A potential employer = formal. 'Dear Mr [Name]' is the correct formal greeting.

content.question: Which sentence is appropriate in a formal email?
options: Sorry for the late reply — things got crazy!
I apologise for the delay in my response.
Oops, forgot to reply — my bad!
content.correct: I apologise for the delay in my response.
content.explanation: Formal English: full sentences, full words, polite phrasing.

content.question: Kavya is messaging her close friend about weekend plans. Which is appropriate?
options: Dear Meera, I am writing to enquire about your availability this weekend.
Hey Meera! Are you free Saturday? 😊
To Whom It May Concern, I wish to discuss weekend plans.
I would be grateful if you could confirm your weekend availability.
content.correct: Hey Meera! Are you free Saturday? 😊
content.explanation: Writing to a close friend = informal. Casual greetings, contractions, and emoji are all fine here.

content.question: Which sign-off is appropriate for a formal email to a client?
content.correct: Best regards, Arjun Mehta
content.explanation: 'Best regards' + full name = professional and appropriate for a client email.

content.instruction: Rewrite the informal phrase as a formal one.

sentences.text: Can you send it ASAP? → Could you please send it ___ ___?
Sorry, forgot to reply → I ___ for the delay in responding.
FYI the meeting is moved. → ___ ___ ___ the meeting has been rescheduled.
Let me know if you need anything. → Please do not ___ to contact me.

content.instruction: Is each email sentence FORMAL (F) or INFORMAL (I)? Write F or I.

sentences.text: I am writing to inform you of a change in schedule.
Hey! Just checking — still on for tomorrow?
I would be grateful if you could review the attached document.
Can't make it today — something came up 😅
I look forward to hearing from you at your earliest convenience.

content.instruction: Arrange into a formal apology sentence.
content.answer: I apologise for the delay in responding.

content.instruction: Arrange into a formal request.
content.answer: I would be grateful if you could confirm your attendance.

content.instruction: Match the informal phrase to its formal equivalent.

pairs.right: at your earliest convenience
Please note that
I apologise for the delay in responding
Could you please review this?
I look forward to hearing from you.

content.title: Two emails — same message, different registers
content.setting: Ravi needs to tell his team about a meeting change. He writes two versions — one to his best friend on the team, and one to his director.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi (to friend Ankit — informal)
dialogue.text: Hey Ankit! Quick heads up — the team meeting tomorrow has moved to 3 PM instead of 11. Something came up on the director's side. Let me know if that works for you. See you then! 😊

dialogue.speaker: Ankit
dialogue.text: No worries! 3 PM works fine. Thanks for letting me know 👍

dialogue.speaker: Ravi (to Director Sharma — formal)
dialogue.text: Dear Mr Sharma, I am writing to inform you that the team meeting scheduled for tomorrow at 11 AM has been rescheduled to 3 PM. I apologise for any inconvenience this may cause. Please let me know if this time is convenient for you. I look forward to your confirmation. Kind regards, Ravi Krishnan

dialogue.speaker: Director Sharma
dialogue.text: Dear Ravi, thank you for the update. 3 PM works for me. Please send a revised calendar invite.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Dear Mr Sharma, thank you for confirming. I will send the updated calendar invite shortly. Kind regards, Ravi

dialogue.speaker: Ravi (to Ankit — informal again)
dialogue.text: Director confirmed! 3 PM it is. I'll update the calendar. This meeting better be short though 😂

dialogue.speaker: Ankit
dialogue.text: Haha agreed! See you tomorrow 🙌

comprehension_questions.question: What changed about the meeting?
options: It moved from 11 AM to 3 PM.
It moved to a different day.
comprehension_questions.correct: It moved from 11 AM to 3 PM.

comprehension_questions.question: Which phrase does Ravi use in the formal email to apologise?
options: I apologise for any inconvenience this may cause.
comprehension_questions.correct: I apologise for any inconvenience this may cause.

comprehension_questions.question: What does the Director ask Ravi to do?

content.instruction: These formal email extracts have register mistakes. Correct them.

sentences.wrong: Hey! I'm writing about the invoice I sent last week.
sentences.correct: Dear [Name], I am writing regarding the invoice I submitted last week.
sentences.explanation: 'Hey' is informal. Use 'Dear'. Also use full form 'I am' and formal verb 'submitted'.

sentences.wrong: Can you get back to me ASAP?
sentences.correct: I would appreciate a response at your earliest convenience.
sentences.explanation: 'ASAP' is informal/abbreviation. 'Can you' is too casual for formal emails.
'Cheers' and emoji are informal. Use 'Kind regards' or 'Yours sincerely' + full name.

content.scenario: You need to email your university's admissions office to ask about scholarship options. Which opening is most appropriate?
options: Hey! I wanted to ask about scholarships.
Dear Admissions Office, I am writing to enquire about scholarship opportunities available for the 2025-26 academic year.
To whoever's reading this — I need scholarship info.
Hi, can you tell me about scholarships?
content.correct: Dear Admissions Office, I am writing to enquire about scholarship opportunities available for the 2025-26 academic year.
content.explanation: An official university department = formal register. Clear, specific, and polite.

content.instruction: Compare these two situations and explain what register you would use and why. Then give 3 example phrases for each.
content.prompt: Situation A: Emailing a new client about a project proposal. Situation B: Messaging your close colleague to say you'll be 10 minutes late. What register? What phrases?
content.example_response:
For Situation A — emailing a new client — I would use formal register because I don't know them personally and this is a professional business context. I would use phrases like: 'Dear Mr/Ms [Name],' to open, 'I am writing to present our proposal for the upcoming project' for the opening line, and 'I look forward to your feedback at your earliest convenience' to close. I would not use contractions or abbreviations.

For Situation B — messaging my close colleague — I would use informal register. We know each other well, this is a quick update, and it's through messaging, not email. I'd write something like: 'Hey! Running 10 mins late — sorry!' or 'On my way — traffic is bad, be there by 9:15' or just 'Late by 10 — sorry!' with maybe a quick emoji. The context matters a lot — the same information is communicated very differently depending on who you're talking to.

content.instruction: Write and say aloud TWO short emails: one formal, one informal — both saying the same thing. The message: you need to cancel a plan for this Friday.
content.prompt: Say aloud: (1) A formal email to a business contact cancelling a Friday meeting. (2) An informal message to a friend cancelling Friday plans.
content.example_response:
Here are both versions:

Formal email: 'Subject: Cancellation of Friday Meeting — 18th October. Dear Ms Kapoor, I am writing to inform you that I am unfortunately unable to attend our meeting scheduled for Friday, 18th October, due to an urgent personal commitment. I sincerely apologise for any inconvenience this may cause. I would be grateful if we could reschedule at a mutually convenient time. Please let me know your availability for next week. Kind regards, Arjun Mehta.'

Informal message to a friend: 'Hey Meera! Really sorry but I have to cancel our Friday plans — something urgent came up with my family. Can we reschedule to next weekend? I'll make it up to you, promise! 🙏 Let me know what works for you.'
key_phrases: I am writing to inform you

content.instruction: Read each context and choose FORMAL or INFORMAL phrasing for each gap.
content.text:
Context: Ravi is writing to his college professor (FORMAL).

Dear ___ (1) Patel,

I ___ (2) writing to request your guidance on the final year project topic. I have been ___ (3) two possible areas — Machine Learning and Data Visualisation — and I would value your expert opinion.

___ (4) you please advise me on which area would be more suitable given the department's current research focus?

I ___ (5) forward to your response. Please ___ (6) hesitate to let me know if you would like me to prepare any additional material beforehand.

___ (7) sincerely,
Ravi Sharma

content.title: Lesson 8 complete — Formal vs informal email
key_points: Register = the level of formality — choose based on your relationship with the reader
Formal: full forms, no slang, polite phrases, professional sign-offs
Informal: contractions, casual phrases, emoji allowed
Common switches: ASAP → 'at your earliest convenience', FYI → 'Please note that', Can you → 'Could you please'
Mixing registers sounds unnatural — be consistent throughout the email
content.next_lesson: Making appointments — suggesting, accepting, and confirming times
